feedback.text: Dear, I would like to criticise the fact that the current EU rules leave far too many gaps, in particular digital purchases and digital ownership. Not only from the point of view of penalising companies or websites and in some cases severely disadvantaged if they want to offer a user-friendly application/webpage or similar digital content compared to alleged rights breakers; EU citizens are also almost entirely left alone for very similar physical goods and digital services, contrary to sometimes different laws, and they remain scams, dangers and partial ‘expropriation’, or at least sudden, unilateral Treaty changes, in most cases without legal certainty or alternative, to take action or appeal against it. I would like to give further, more concrete examples, but I have already seen some good explanations and cases listed by other EU citizens on this feedback platform, so I would simply like to join them and confirm that many of these complaints correspond to the facts in general. In summary, in my view, the current legislation is a clear and serious gap in EU law, which is closed. The internet has now existed for decades and it can be expected that it will address an important and critical regulation affecting every EU citizen, much faster and more seriously. Looking at the last 10 years alone and looking at the discussion, regulations and commissioned studies of the EU institutions on the internet, it seems clear that decision-makers also seem to have come to the fore that ‘volunteering and self-regulation by industry’ usually goes hand in hand with the loss of the rights of EU citizens. I therefore call for the establishment of grey areas, in particular as regards the ownership, supporter of digital content and the right to lizen, in such a way that legal certainty exists once for all and that the EU’s cultural and social achievements are reflected in this return. It cannot be that acts such as loans, a simple and natural human activity, which has been documented and practised for millennia, is physically possible at any time, especially among family members, but is suddenly made impossible in the digital environment or for the sole benefit of digital giants. He said that the EU was better.
